content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441

October 8, 1981


Uvaldo H. Palomares
Member
California State Board of Education
721 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA 95814

A-81-10-062

Dear Mr. Palomares:

This letter is sent in response to your request for
advice as to your duties as a member of the State Board of
Education under the Political Reform Act. The following
information and discussion should give you general guidance
in determining whether or not your disqualification is required
as to State Board decisions; however, if you are unsure or
would like further advice as to a particular decision or
decisions, we will be happy to provide you with advice pursuant
to Government Code Section 83114(b).

My understanding of the situation from your letter and
our discussion is as follows. You are the majority shareholder
of Human Development Training Institute, a California corporation.
The Institute contracts with school districts and county
offices of education throughout the United States with a
minor but significant amount of business in California.
Generally these contracts are to supply textbooks and other
instructional materials, to conduct in-service training
including the giving of speeches for the motivation and
training of teachers and administrators, and to provide
technical assistance by identifying and recommending solutions
to various educational or administrative problems. Usually
the contracts are obtained through a bidding process for the
particular project or assignment.


1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code.

Uvaldo H. Palomares
October 8, 1981
Page Two


The Political Reform Act states that:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

                                        Section 87100.

     An official has a financial interest when it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on any source of income of $250 or more within the
prior 12 months received by or promised to the official.
Section 87103.  The act defines income to include "a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in which
the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater."  Section 82030(a).

     Assuming you have received more than $250 within the
past 12 months from the Institute, the Institute is a "source
of income" to you within the meaning of Section 87103, and
you must refrain from participating in decisions which would
foreseeably have a material financial effect on the Institute.
This would include decisions on the funding of projects in
which the Institute is playing a role and any other decisions
from which there will foreseeably be either a profit or loss
to the Institute.

     In addition, since you own a greater than 10 percent
interest in the Institute, sources of income to the Institute,
of which your pro rata share is $250 or more, are also sources
of income to you within the meaning of Section 87103.  Accordingly,
you must also refrain from participating in decisions which
would foreseeably have a material financial effect different
from their effect on the public generally on those school
districts or county offices of education with which the
Institute has contracted.  Sections 87100 and 87103.

     In your letter, you listed several types of decisions
that the Board makes and questioned whether these decisions
could foreseeably have a material financial effect on the
school districts.  Section 87103 states that even where a
decision has a foreseeable material financial effect on an
official's investments, interest in real property or sources
of income, an official does not have to disqualify himself
or herself from participating in the decision if the effect

Uvaldo H. Palomares
October 8, 1981
Page Three


on the official's interests is indistinguishable from the effects of the decision on the public generally.  The exception to the rule of disqualifications for decisions which affect the official's interests and they affect the public or a significant segment thereof is narrowly construed.  However, an effect that is felt similarly by all or most school districts is an effect felt by the "public generally" within the meaning of Section 87103.  School districts are public agencies which cover the entire state and represent the varied interests of the state's population.  The Board's primary function is to oversee and regulate the activities of school districts.  Therefore, you need not disqualify yourself from participating in decisions of the Board which will affect all school districts, or a significant segment thereof, in a similar manner.

If, however, a decision involves only one district, or a small number of districts, and one or more of these districts is a "source of income" to you, you will have to disqualify yourself from participating if the decision has financial significance for the district.  Section 87103.

If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

CALIFORNIA STATE BOARD OF EDUCATION
721 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814


July 28, 1981


Mr. Ted Prim
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Prim:

The purpose of this letter is to request the informal advice of your staff on the
subject of whether I, as a member of the State Board of Education, should abstain
from voting on any of the following items which appear on the State Board Agenda.

Preliminarily, I should note that the State Board of Education is created in
Article IX of the California Constitution and consists of ten members appointed
by the Governor with the concurrence of the Senate. The interests required to be
disclosed by the State Board to be disclosed in the annual disclosure statement are set forth
in Category I of Section 18600 of Title 5, California Administrative Code, a copy
of which is attached.

You should also note that I am majority shareholder of the Human Development
Training Institute, which is a private profit-making California corporation, en-
gaged in several different kinds of activities under contract with school districts
and county offices of Education throughout the United States with a minor but
significant amount of business in California. These activities can be generally
described as follows:

(1) The company produces textbooks and other instructional materials which are
    sold on a direct basis to school districts. None of these materials are
    submitted to or evaluated or approved by the State Board of Education.
    Some of these materials, however, are purchased by schools with the cash
    allotment provided for in Education Code Section 60242, subdivision (b).

(2) The company provides in-service training to school districts who are ex-
    periencing various kinds of program or management problems. For example, I
    and my employees often give speeches in districts for the motivation and
    training of teachers and administrators.

Page 2
Mr. Ted Prim
July 28, 1981


(3) The company provides technical assistance to school districts and county
offices of education to identify different kinds of educational or administrative problems and to recommend solutions to those problems.

In most instances the company bids on and obtains a contract with the school district
for the particular project or assignment.

As a member of the State Board of Education, I am called upon to vote on various kinds
of items. These may be grouped generally into the following categories:

(1) Those items which apply generally to all school districts in the state. An
example of such an item is regulations governing education programs. These
items are generally nonfinancial in nature. To the extent that the
regulations impose fiscal costs or benefits on school districts, they do so
generally. The fiscal effect upon districts which my company has contracted
with or could contract with, as distinguished from school districts generally,
could not reasonably be anticipated.

(2) Those items which apply to a relatively large number of identified districts.
For example, the State Board of Education has established a single application
form for federal and state education programs. The Board has established
criteria for the approval of these applications, and the Department brings to
the Board for approval the names of those districts whose applications meet
the criteria. In July, the Board, in one motion, approved such applications
for funding for 612 school districts. With some effort, I could determine
with which of the districts my company has had contracts in the past. However,
I have no way of knowing which, if any, of the districts proposing in their applications to utilize the services of outside consultants or, if so, whether any of
such services could be provided by my company.

(3) Those items which apply to a relatively small number of identified districts.
In addition to the above example, there was one school district whose application for funding was contested by the community advisory council established
in the district. The Board was called upon to evaluate the validity of this
contest and make a judgment as to whether the application for funding should
be approved or denied. As in the case of category (2), the amount of money
available to the district is not discretionary with the Board; the issue is
whether the Board should approve the education program described in the
application. The amount of funding is determined by statutory formulas.

(4) Those items in which school districts are competing for funds. There are two
or three federal programs in which the allocation of funds to districts and
counties depends upon a competitive application process. In this instance,
the Board has established criteria for the evaluation of the applications, and
the Department of Education brings to the Board applications of those districts
which have received the highest scores in the review process. The Board does
have the discretion, to the extent that it is not arbitrary or capricious, to
affect funding to a district by changing the rank order of districts awarded
funds.

Page 3
Mr. Ted Prim
July 28, 1981




(5) Those items such as school district reorganizations, which apply to a
single district and tangentially affect funding. From time to time the
Board receives and must act upon petitions to reorganize school districts.
Generally, these involve either the unification of existing districts into
a larger new district or the splitting off of territory from an existing
district to form a new second district. In the first example, the existing
districts cease to exist, and a new district takes their place; in the
second example, the existing districts remain with a smaller number of
students. The decision of the Board, if approved by the electorate of the
district, will not change the amount of state funding in total but would
affect the amount of funding to each component district.

Because of these items arise with some regularity, your prompt attention to this
request for advice would be appreciated. If you need further information, please
feel free to call either me at area code 714-233-7023 or the Chief Counsel for the
State Department of Education, Tom Griffin at 445-4694.


Sincerely,


[signature]
UVALDO H. PALOMARES
Member


UP/gaj

cc: Diane M. Fishburn
    Legal Counsel

TITLE 5 BOARD OF EDUCATION PROCEDURES § 15600
(Register 81, No. 15-4-11-81) (p. 423-267)

CHAPTER 5 STATE BOARD OF EDUCATION—
CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE

DETAILED ANALYSIS

Section
15600 General Provisions
Appendix

18600. General Provisions.
The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000, et seq., requires
state and local government agencies to adopt and promulgate Conflict of Interest Codes. The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a regulation, 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, which contains the terms of a standard Conflict
of Interest Code, which can be incorporated by reference and which may be
amended by the Fair Political Practices Commission to conform to amendments in the Political Reform Act after public notice and hearings. Therefore,
the terms of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730 and any amendments to it duly
adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission, along with the attached
Appendix in which officials and employees are designated and disclosure categories are set forth, are hereby incorporated by reference and constitute the
Conflict of Interest Code of the State Board of Education, except as provided
below.

Pursuant to Section 4(A) of the standard Code, designated employees shall
file statements of economic interests with the agency. Upon receipt of the
statements of members of the Board, the agency shall make and retain a copy
and forward the original of these statements to the Fair Political Practices
Commission.

EXCEPTION As provided in Section 1 of the standard Code, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18730(b)(1), the definitions contained in the Political Reform Act of 1974 shall apply to
the terms used in this Code except that neither the term "investment" nor the term
"business entity" shall operate to exclude any private school in California, whether or not
such school is operated for profit.

NOTE Authority cited: Sections 87300 and 87304, Government Code. Reference: Sections
87300, et seq., Government Code.

HISTORY.
1. New Chapter 5 (Sections 15600-15608) filed 12-17-76, designated effective 2-1-77.
Approved by Fair Political Practices Commission 10-6-76 (Register 76, No. 51).
2. Amendment of NOTE filed 9-23-77, effective thirtieth day thereafter (Register 77,
No. 39).
3. Repealer of Chapter 5 (Sections 15600-15608) and new Chapter 5 (Sections 15600 and
Appendix) filed 2-25-81, effective thirtieth day thereafter. Approved by Fair Political
Practices Commission 12-1-80 (Register 81, No. 9).

APPENDIX

Designated Employees                    Disclosure
                                        Category
Members of the State Board of Education          I
All persons employed by the State Board of Education in other than clerical positions     II

§ 13600
(D. 528.288)

BOARD OF EDUCATION PROCEDURES

TITLE 5

(Register 81, No. 15—4/11/81)

Disclosure Categories

Category I Persons in this category shall report:
(1) Investments, business positions, and income to the extent that they know
or have reason to know that the business entity in which the investment or
business position is held or the source of income is a publisher, manufacturer,
vendor of instructional materials, or services offered to educational institutions or libraries in the State of California. They shall also report any investments, positions of management and income from any private school in the
State of California.
(2) Interests in real property located in a district either currently being
reorganized or being considered for reorganization at the closing date of the
statement.

Category II Persons in this category shall report:
(1) Interests in real property;
(2) Investments, business positions and income from any private school in
the State of California; and
(3) Investments, business positions and income to the extent they know or
have reason to know that the business entity in which the investment or business position is held or the source of income is:
(a) A publisher, manufacturer, or vendor of instructional materials, educational materials, or consulting services offered to educational institutions or
libraries in the State of California;
(b) Engaged in the performance of educational, research or professional
consulting services of the type utilized by the State Board of Education, or
(c) One which manufactures or sells supplies, books, machines, or equipment of the type utilized by those units for which they have responsibility.

(Next page is 428.271)

82274 C—250 4/81 3,000

sections.question: Whether as a member of the State Board of Education, Mr. Palomares should abstain from voting on various items appearing on the State Board agenda, given that he is the majority shareholder of Human Development Training Institute, a private corporation that contracts with school districts and county offices of education throughout the United States with business in California to provide textbooks, instructional materials, in-service training, and technical assistance.
sections.conclusion: You must refrain from participating in decisions which would foreseeably have a material financial effect on the Human Development Training Institute. This includes decisions on funding of projects in which the Institute is playing a role and decisions affecting school districts or county offices with which the Institute has contracted. However, you need not disqualify yourself from decisions affecting all school districts or a significant segment thereof in a similar manner, as the effect would be indistinguishable from effects on the public generally. Disqualification is required only when a decision involves one district or a small number of districts, one or more of which is a source of income to you, and the decision has financial significance for that district.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a State Board of Education member disqualify himself from Board decisions when he holds a majority interest in a company that contracts with school districts that may be affected by those decisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Palomares must disqualify himself from Board decisions that foreseeably have material financial effects on the Institute or on specific school districts with which it has contracted, but need not disqualify himself from decisions affecting all or most school districts equally.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (July 28, 1981) from Mr. Palomares and the FPPC response letter (October 8, 1981) from Diane Maura Fishburn. Extracted information pertains to the FPPC response as primary document. Both letters are included in the source text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether as a member of the State Board of Education, Mr. Palomares should abstain from voting on various items appearing on the State Board agenda, given that he is the majority shareholder of Human Development Training Institute, a private corporation that contracts with school districts and county offices of education throughout the United States with business in California to provide textbooks, instructional materials, in-service training, and technical assistance.

CONCLUSION: You must refrain from participating in decisions which would foreseeably have a material financial effect on the Human Development Training Institute. This includes decisions on funding of projects in which the Institute is playing a role and decisions affecting school districts or county offices with which the Institute has contracted. However, you need not disqualify yourself from decisions affecting all school districts or a significant segment thereof in a similar manner, as the effect would be indistinguishable from effects on the public generally. Disqualification is required only when a decision involves one district or a small number of districts, one or more of which is a source of income to you, and the decision has financial significance for that district.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises State Board of Education member Uvaldo Palomares regarding his disqualification obligations under the Political Reform Act given his majority ownership of Human Development Training Institute, which contracts with school districts. The FPPC concludes he must disqualify himself from decisions affecting specific districts that are sources of income to him, but not from decisions affecting all or most districts similarly.
